corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: In this work, we encode the graph structure directly using a neural network model f ( X, A ) and train on a supervised target L 0 for all nodes with labels, thereby avoiding explicit graph-based regularization in the loss function.

location.sentence: As outlined in the in- troduction, we can relax certain assumptions typically made in graph-based semi-supervised learn- ing by conditioning our model f ( X, A ) both on the data X and on the adjacency matrix A of the underlying graph structure.

context_window.previous: 3 S EMI -S UPERVISED N ODE C LASSIFICATION Having introduced a simple, yet flexible model f ( X, A ) for efficient information propagation on graphs, we can return to the problem of semi-supervised node classification.
context_window.current: As outlined in the in- troduction, we can relax certain assumptions typically made in graph-based semi-supervised learn- ing by conditioning our model f ( X, A ) both on the data X and on the adjacency matrix A of the underlying graph structure.
context_window.next: We expect this setting to be especially powerful in scenarios where the adjacency matrix contains information not present in the data X , such as citation links between doc- uments in a citation network or relations in a knowledge graph.

location.sentence: A well-studied framework for the unique assignment of node labels given a graph and (optionally) dis- crete initial node labels is provided by the 1-dim Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL-1) algorithm (Weisfeiler & Lehmann, 1968): Algorithm 1: WL-1 algorithm (Weisfeiler & Lehmann, 1968) Input: Initial node coloring ( h (0) 1 , h (0) 2 , ..., h (0) N ) Output: Final node coloring ( h ( T ) 1 , h ( T ) 2 , ..., h ( T ) N ) t ← 0; repeat for v i ∈ V do h ( t +1) i ← hash ( ∑ j ∈N i h ( t ) j ) ; t ← t + 1 ; until stable node coloring is reached ; Here, h ( t ) i denotes the coloring (label assignment) of node v i (at iteration t ) and N i is its set of neighboring node indices (irrespective of whether the graph includes self-connections for every node or not). hash( · ) is a hash function.

context_window.previous: 10 Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2017 A R ELATION TO W EISFEILER -L EHMAN A LGORITHM A neural network model for graph-structured data should ideally be able to learn representations of nodes in a graph, taking both the graph structure and feature description of nodes into account.
context_window.current: A well-studied framework for the unique assignment of node labels given a graph and (optionally) dis- crete initial node labels is provided by the 1-dim Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL-1) algorithm (Weisfeiler & Lehmann, 1968): Algorithm 1: WL-1 algorithm (Weisfeiler & Lehmann, 1968) Input: Initial node coloring ( h (0) 1 , h (0) 2 , ..., h (0) N ) Output: Final node coloring ( h ( T ) 1 , h ( T ) 2 , ..., h ( T ) N ) t ← 0; repeat for v i ∈ V do h ( t +1) i ← hash ( ∑ j ∈N i h ( t ) j ) ; t ← t + 1 ; until stable node coloring is reached ; Here, h ( t ) i denotes the coloring (label assignment) of node v i (at iteration t ) and N i is its set of neighboring node indices (irrespective of whether the graph includes self-connections for every node or not). hash( · ) is a hash function.

location.sentence: This problem can be framed as graph-based semi-supervised learning, where label information is smoothed over the graph via some form of explicit graph-based regularization (Zhu et al., 2003; Zhou et al., 2004; Belkin et al., 2006; Weston et al., 2012), e.g. by using a graph Laplacian regularization term in the loss function: L = L 0 + λ L reg , with L reg = ∑ i,j A ij ‖ f ( X i ) − f ( X j ) ‖ 2 = f ( X ) > ∆ f ( X ) .

top_candidates.sentence: Here, we describe several limitations of our current model and outline how these might be overcome in future work.
To alleviate this problem, we introduce the following renormalization trick: , with Ã = A + I N and Dii = j Ãij .

A well-studied framework for the unique assignment of node labels given a graph and (optionally) discrete initial node labels is provided by the 1-dim Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL-1) algorithm ): Algorithm 1: WL-1 algorithm Input: Initial node coloring (h denotes the coloring (label assignment) of node v i (at iteration t) and N i is its set of neighboring node indices (irrespective of whether the graph includes self-connections for every node or not). hash(•) is a hash function.

A well-studied framework for the unique assignment of node labels given a graph and (optionally) discrete initial node labels is provided by the 1-dim Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL-1) algorithm ): Algorithm 1: WL-1 algorithm Input: Initial node coloring (h denotes the coloring (label assignment) of node v i (at iteration t) and N i is its set of neighboring node indices (irrespective of whether the graph includes self-connections for every node or not). hash(•) is a hash function.
For an in-depth mathematical discussion of the WL-1 algorithm see, e.g., .

We can replace the hash function in Algorithm 1 with a neural network layer-like differentiable function with trainable parameters as follows: where c ij is an appropriately chosen normalization constant for the edge (v i , v j ).
This-loosely speaking-allows us to interpret our GCN model as a differentiable and parameterized generalization of the 1-dim Weisfeiler-Lehman algorithm on graphs.
Label rate denotes the number of labeled nodes that are used for training divided by the total number of nodes in each dataset.
NELL is a bipartite graph dataset extracted from a knowledge graph with 55,864 relation nodes and 9,891 entity nodes.
NELL NELL is a dataset extracted from the knowledge graph introduced in .

We present a scalable approach for semi-supervised learning on graph-structured data that is based on an efficient variant of convolutional neural networks which operate directly on graphs.
We motivate the choice of our convolutional architecture via a localized first-order approximation of spectral graph convolutions.
In a number of experiments on citation networks and on a knowledge graph dataset we demonstrate that our approach outperforms related methods by a significant margin.
In a number of experiments on citation networks and on a knowledge graph dataset we demonstrate that our approach outperforms related methods by a significant margin.

Firstly, we introduce a simple and well-behaved layer-wise propagation rule for neural network models which operate directly on graphs and show how it can be motivated from a first-order approximation of spectral graph convolutions .

Experiments on a number of datasets demonstrate that our model compares favorably both in classification accuracy and efficiency (measured in wall-clock time) against state-of-the-art methods for semi-supervised learning.

In the experiments demonstrated here, our method for semi-supervised node classification outperforms recent related methods by a significant margin.
